seoTitle: Add Form 8949 in TurboTax: Report Capital Gains
metaDescription: Learn how to add Form 8949 in TurboTax to report stock and bond sales correctly, avoid common entry mistakes, and stay compliant for your 2026 return.
intro: Form 8949 is where TurboTax records the details behind your capital gains and losses, from stock sales to bond transactions. If you sold investments this year, getting this form right matters for both accuracy and your final refund. This guide walks through where to find it in TurboTax and how to enter your transactions without tripping over common errors.

steps.heading: Open the investment income section
steps.body: In TurboTax, navigate to the income section and select the area for stocks, bonds, and other investments — this is where Form 8949 entries begin.

steps.heading: Enter or import your transactions
steps.body: Add each capital asset sale manually, or import your brokerage statement if TurboTax supports your provider, so gains and losses populate automatically.

steps.heading: Review gains and losses for accuracy
steps.body: Double-check purchase dates, sale dates, and cost basis for each transaction, since these determine whether a sale counts as short-term or long-term.

steps.heading: Confirm how Form 8949 flows to Schedule D
steps.body: TurboTax automatically summarizes your Form 8949 entries onto Schedule D, so review the summary before moving on to make sure totals look right.

faq.question: Do I need Form 8949 if I only have a few stock sales?
faq.answer: Yes — any sale of a capital asset like stocks or bonds generally needs to be reported on Form 8949, regardless of how many transactions you have.

faq.question: Can TurboTax import my brokerage data automatically?
faq.answer: Many brokerages support direct import into TurboTax, which can save time and reduce manual entry errors, though you should still verify the imported figures.

faq.question: What happens if I enter the wrong cost basis?
faq.answer: An incorrect cost basis can overstate or understate your gain, so it's worth cross-checking against your official brokerage statements before filing.

faq.question: Is Form 8949 included in TurboTax's free version?
faq.answer: Investment reporting typically requires a paid TurboTax tier, since it involves more complex forms than the free version usually covers.
